does: This paper develops a two-index anchor-and-resume concession framework for automated freight rate negotiation under dynamic pricing. The core contribution is a method that derives the concession shape parameter β from live pricing spreads to adapt negotiation posture in real time, while a novel anchor-and-resume mechanism guarantees monotonically non-decreasing offers even when pricing targets shift mid-conversation. Unlike previous approaches that rely on expensive LLM reasoning for pricing decisions, this framework keeps all pricing logic deterministic and formulaic, relegating language models to natural-language translation only. The method is evaluated on 115,125 real freight negotiations, demonstrating that the adaptive β tailors concession behavior appropriately to each load's margin structure while maintaining offer monotonicity—a critical constraint in negotiation credibility.
connects: This work extends classical time-dependent concession models from negotiation theory, which traditionally used fixed β parameters, into the domain of dynamic repricing where target prices change during ongoing negotiations. It responds to limitations of recent LLM-augmented negotiation systems that rely on expensive model calls for each pricing decision and suffer from non-determinism and prompt-injection vulnerabilities. The paper builds on established game-theoretic negotiation frameworks while incorporating practical constraints from high-volume freight brokerage operations. It connects to research on automated negotiation agents, mechanism design under incomplete information, and the challenge of integrating neural models into decision-making pipelines while maintaining interpretability and reliability.
enables: This framework opens several directions for future work in automated negotiation. The separation of deterministic pricing logic from language understanding suggests a cleaner architecture for other negotiation domains where dynamic repricing occurs. The anchor-and-resume mechanism could extend to multi-issue negotiations or hierarchical negotiation strategies. The demonstrated success at scale (115k negotiations) enables deployment in real freight operations and provides a testbed for studying how algorithmic negotiators interact with human carriers over time. The approach also points toward methods for integrating foundation models into high-stakes decision systems while maintaining safety guarantees and avoiding expensive inference, potentially applicable beyond negotiation to other sequential decision-making tasks requiring NLP interfaces.
